text: The surgeon navigates the surgical field, developing the peritoneal flap and identifying weak indirect and direct hernia spots, as well as an early femoral hernia. Using retraction with the left hand, the surgeon exposes areolar tissue and the inferior epigastric vessels. The dissection continues below the arcuate line, separating fibroareolar tissue from the peritoneum, with visible transverse fascia typically seen in direct hernias.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum away from the testicular vessels, revealing the direct portion of the hernia. The transverse salus fascia is visible as it is separated from the hernia contents. The surgeon works broadly from left to right, avoiding working in a confined space. A small accidental hole is made in the hernia sac, which is minimal in size. The femoral hernia is being reduced, with visible fat in the hernia separate from the obturator fat located more to the right. The surgeon crosses the midline during the dissection, exposing an indentation in the direct space covered by fat.

The surgeon carefully dissects in a plane where the bladder separates cleanly from surrounding tissues, ensuring a hemostatic technique to maintain clear visibility. The peritoneum is hugged closely to leave a protective fascial layer over the pelvic nerves, including the lateral femoral cutaneous and femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve. The mesh is positioned to cover the posterior aspect effectively, transitioning from the retro-pubic space into the space of Retzius without interference from the bladder or peritoneum. The surgeon identifies and maintains the separation of fat from the bladder fat, indicating the desired surgical plane.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis, providing a clear view of the surgical field, including the full bladder, which aids in visualizing its curvature. The dissection is performed thoroughly to ensure no structures are left beneath the mesh. The obturator nerve becomes visible, appearing more medial than usual, indicating the completion of the dissection.

The surgeon uses an extra-large mesh to cover the surgical area, moving to the left side to perform a similar dissection. The right-handed use of scissors affects the flap development. A fenestrated bipolar instrument aids in retraction and plane separation. The surgeon stays outside the retro-rectus space, aiming to reach the peritoneum quickly. Various microfacial planes are visible, and the surgeon connects the dissection to the other side, focusing on staying close to the peritoneum to navigate between the testicular vessels, peritoneum, and hernia sac.

The surgeon performs a precise dissection on a thin patient, revealing minimal fat between tissue planes. The transverse salus fascia is visible, resembling a direct inguinal hernia. The hernia is lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels, connecting with the medial part of the hernia. The medial aspect of the hernia is exposed, with the inferior epigastric vessels floating in front of the defect, which is separated in a pantaloon fashion.

The surgeon retracts tissue to the eight or nine o'clock position to unstack layers for better visibility. Using scissors like a hot scalpel, the surgeon dissects through the tissue, revealing a true internal ring with core structures. The defect is separate from the internal ring, and the surgeon aims to achieve the same plane on the left side as on the right, exposing the superior pubic rami. Fat is carefully dissected, distinguishing between different colors to avoid dividing fat containing obturator lymph nodes.

The surgeon prioritizes the cord while hugging the peritoneum, carefully avoiding the vas and surrounding autonomic nerves. Medial retraction is applied to move tissue away from the vas, iliac vein, and obturator artery, nerve, and vein, aiding in finding the correct dissection plane. Despite maintaining hemostasis, some bleeding occurs, complicating the dissection. The surgeon continues to hug the peritoneum, mindful of the bowel on the opposite side, and cauterizes closer to the peritoneum to manage bleeding.

The surgeon completes the dissection with the aid of the convexity of the full bladder, which helps in determining its position. The scissor is exchanged for a mega suture cut needle driver, and the mesh is positioned with a vertical blue line aimed at the vas. The mesh is tucked vertically into the retro pubic space, ensuring excellent overlap laterally and posteriorly, with no chance of peritoneum slipping behind. The mesh provides two to three centimeters of overlap above the weak transverse arch.

The surgeon secures the mesh to Cooper's ligament with a suture to prevent it from lifting, despite it being well-tucked. An additional suture is placed medially due to the dilated floor, using a 3-0 Vicryl cut to ten inches for multiple uses. The operation proceeds efficiently with maintained hemostasis.

The surgeon efficiently sutures the mesh into the retro pubic space, ensuring it remains vertical and stable when the bladder fills. The vertical line of the mesh is positioned over the vas, providing excellent coverage without peritoneum tucked behind. The posterior lateral edge of the mesh is aligned against the cord structures. Two stitches are placed to secure the mesh to the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, preventing rotation and maintaining position as the abdomen is desufflated.

The surgeon completes the mesh placement and begins closing the defect using a 3-0 V-Lock suture. The suture is efficiently handled, with the needle consistently ready in the right hand to minimize wasted movement. The left hand pulls the needle out, and the wrist is rotated to present the needle to the right hand, ensuring smooth operation. The surgeon double backs two throws to lock the suture in place and carefully cuts the tail without tension to prevent bowel obstruction.

The surgeon uses a short suture to close the peritoneal flap from lateral to medial, carefully avoiding tearing the lower flap. The left hand presents the needle to the right hand for efficient suturing with a six-inch V-lock, allowing multiple throws before pulling through. As the pneumoperitoneum pressure is lowered, the surgeon holds the peritoneum up to prevent the flap from collapsing. After closing the hole, the pressure is increased to ensure the mesh is properly positioned without posterior lifting.
